author: @garygranato9164
text: great vid , what flux did you use robert ?

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Likely he used solder with flux in the core.

author: @flashlit123
text:
The solder used here is a special type called C-Solder and as far as I can see it is very expensive.  I still wonder if normal electrical solder could be used if the ink was zinc or copper plated first.


According to wikipedia, C-Solder is Sn90.7Ag3.6Cu0.7Cr5

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it could be mate - there is a specific solder for zinc and it flows well

author: @gilohadmanskie1771
text:
awesome...in so many ways......answering this user question by plane filmed empiric data collection...I felt like i was just a casual witness to one of your daily and normal laboratory tests....   so openly performed, felt like even if the ink had not shown its ability to be soldered you would have published it/uploaded it, just for the sake of having answered the question...in these days of polished/edited/compressed/photoshopped/smoothed out Biz--Vid-Materials a really really contrasting approach/impression you leave. Thumbs up.
By the way:

Could you make an educated guess concerning the transition resistance from one medium to the other, from SnPbAg-solder to Graphene-ink?
Is there aconductor combination to which it could be compared concerning the transition resistances? Thnx for being and teaching

author: @petermiddleton6902
text: Awesome stuff! :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @VioleRose100
text:
Dear Sir Smith 
How can i contact you.
I have a Graphite Schist from natural source. A cousin works in mining company provided me. So i would like to see the possibility of expansion and methodology. 
Can i contact you by email?

author: @lisakingscott7729
text: So what temperature will the binder in the FWG ink withstand? I presume that modern high tin solder will cause tin - carbon bonds, with some tin carbide in the joint? Older lead based solders would presumably be more problematic as lead carbide is only hypothetical?

author: @KJackskii
text: If i join the membership will there be more in depth discussion on making the milk hemp plastic?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there isn't yet but it is a good idea for a video for sure - I'll get on it

replies.author: @KJackskii
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering thank you so much! The recipes I have are jeopardized by water and either shrink or crack or disintegrate and this is the only one ive found impenetrable by water for at least a little while.

author: @Larryloafer488
text:
Robert, deepest apologies of gate crashing this video, but I have no other way to contact you. We are in need of your help, can you spare a few minutes?

https://youtu.be/__5aCCZZqDU

Much appreciated in advance.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @amazac2078
text: Hiya ROB, excellent try more wonderful issues about your ink ,nice of ya to share with us,i think what happened ! there was the hot molten solder  bound to the resin or glue that you've developed ,i guess the tear or crack that might happened later is subject to the (glue) strength,if using a through hole where the connection is!  and paint the sheet from the other side>  the size of the terminal (connection point ) then solder the wire or component from both sides it will be long life connection and less tear or cracks might occured ,cheers mate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: personally mate - I think it is easier to mechanically fix - but folks wanted to know if you could solder to it

replies.author: @amazac2078
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Hiya Rob,no doubt about it never crossed to my mind,the hint was great eep the good works bro,God bless.

author: @bridgendesar
text: Because I'm just dipping my toe into 3d printing, I wonder how that stuff would print as a filament, thus enabling rapid prototyping of a pcb?

replies.author: @bridgendesar

author: @BurnabyAlex
text:
That's quite impressive.   I would have thought you would have had to pre-treat the solder area with your Wand Electroplating.  

I wonder what the chemistry of the carbon-lead junction looks like.    could other atoms with a similar atomic radius get stuck into the carbon, such as Neon, or Boron.  
What if you coated tungsten with graphene?  Would that bring a tungsten electrode's temperature rating up a thousand degrees kelvin?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a really interesting point mate - I must see if I can get time to look at that

author: @martinjandijkstra3205
text: Impressive!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @TheBodgybrothers
text: Still don't understand the chemical bond of the metal to the carbon.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's not chemical mate - it's a wetting phenomenon and a mechanical bond

replies.author: @lisakingscott7729
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering So you're basically saying the bond is due to molecular entanglement? Not due to any chemical effects, Van der Waal's forces etc...?

replies.author: @TheTinkersDam
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Not sure if you're still checking these comments but I hope you are. I'm working on a project in which direct soldering to graphene would be a preferable alternative. I've tried all of the solders in my kit and none of them will bond. They're all lead-based. Here in the future, google AI says that silver solder should do the trick. What type of solder did you use here? Thanks so much.

author: @aaaricmondroofing1703
text:
Can probably put copper nano particles for Evan better bond. Well thanks to RMS we can do that to .
Thanks again guys

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @kreynolds1123
text: That is great! Word of caution though. One will still have to be careful with it physically. Even soldering wires to a PCB (printed  circuit board), can pull up metal foil traces.  It will be a good thing to work in strain relief to protect the solder joint.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate - to be honest I think I would just mechanically fix it

author: @DavidKenny64
text: Did you try testing the mechanical connection?  In other words, how much pull will it take for the joint to fail?

replies.author: @aaaricmondroofing1703
replies.text: David Kenny  RMS pulled out the ditch last week. We will be painting and soldering a suspension bridge across the pond this week to cut shipping cost .

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: Even wires soldered to a PCB need to be secured or have some kind of strain relief. As metal foils can be easily ripped up. I'd bet the same precautions need to be observed here.

replies.author: @DavidKenny64
replies.text: I'm not knocking anything here.  Big fan of the channel.  I'm just curious if it can be bumped off easily, or if it can survive a bit of a pull.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I didn't really test it that much mate - I picked the board up by the wire and dangled it a bit but that was all - personally I would mechanically fix every time - but it was cool it soldered

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol

author: @JSabh
text: I love how enthusiastic you are. To be honest, I knew it would work because I know that you have accounted for heat related issues already when you and your team were developing the ink several years ago. That and, obviously, conductive material will work with other conductive material lol but I do wonder how the heat transfer would look through a thermal camera. Kind of a ink vs solder heat experiment. Cheers my friend.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that would be cool to see for sure

author: @kennedy67951
text: If you consider all this in your design work first. You could lay out a very fine copper mesh matrices first. Then applying your Ink product over top the copper matrixes of micro wires giving your product a more stable electric signal and less of a failure rate incase of solder pad liftage.  Burnishing these areas may increase electrical conductivity. What is your opinion here ROBERT? Thank you for your time ROBERT. It's very appreciated by me anyway.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: They are good ideas mate - but I tend towards trying to simplify - to be honest I think the best bonding method here is simply to crimp or bolt onto it

author: @aga5897
text:
Cool !
Seeing as it conducts, you could always copper plate bits if you like, making soldering easier.
Worked for me ;) http://www.sciencemadness.org/talk/viewthread.php?tid=70570&page=7#pid470105

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like dit mate but to be honest I would still just crimp to it or stick a bolt through
